coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of B, S, and N-doped glucose carbon dots: Antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant activity

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are prepared using glucose as a carbon source (GCD) and doped with heteroatoms such as boron, sulfur, and nitrogen to enhance their functionality. FTIR and XPS results confirm doping of GCDs with heteroatoms, and their particle size is ~10 nm, as shown by TEM images. GCDs are very hydrophilic and stable in an aqueous solution, and nitrogen-doped GCD (NGCD) exhibits the highest polydispersity index of 0.274. The GCDs show excellent tunability of the PL emission with the variation in excitation wavelength. All the GCDs display excellent antioxidant function with the highest activity in the NGCD. The boron-doped GCD (BGCD) and sulfur-doped GCD (SGCD) exhibit more potent antibacterial activity against E. coli and Listeria monocytogenes. The NGCD is effective against both bacterial strains, showing the highest antibacterial activity. The NGCD exhibits potent antifungal activity against Ammophilus fumigatus, P. citrinum, C. albicans, and R. rubra, while the SGCD has a higher inhibitory effect on the growth of F. solani. Besides, more than 80% of the mouse fibroblast L929 cells are viable even when exposed to a high concentration of 500 μg/mL for 72 h, indicating low toxicity.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the glucose-based CDs: TEM images and particle size distribution of (a) GCD, (b) BGCD, (c) SGCD, and (d) NGCD. (e) XPS spectra of GCDs. (f) FTIR spectra of glucose and GCDs.
(a) The UV–vis light absorption spectra of glucose and CDs and emission spectra of the (b) GCD, (c) BGCD, (d) SGCD, and (e) NGCD.
Antioxidant activity (ABTS and DPPH scavenging assay) of the CDs at different concentrations and absorption spectra of ABTS and DPPH solutions with varying concentrations of GCDs.
Schematic representation of (a) Gram-positive and (b) Gram-negative bacteria and (c) fungal cell wall components.

Results of the disk diffusion assay of GCDs and their quantitative data of inhibition diameter against bacteria (Listeria monocytogenes and Escherichia coli), mold (Aspergillus fumigatus, Fusarium solani, and Penicillium citrinum), and yeast (Candida albicans and Rhodotorula rubra).
SEM images of Listeria monocytogenes and E. coli treated with GCDs and ROS generation efficiency of GCDs after a one-hour exposure to sodium fluorescein in alkaline conditions.
Viability of mouse fibroblast L929 cells after 72 h treatment with different concentrations of glucose carbon dots (GCD) and doped glucose carbon dots (BGCD, SGCD, and NGCD) as calculated from MTT.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of GCDs.
Antimicrobial activity of GCDs in terms of MIC/MBC or MFC values.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of B, S, and N-doped glucose carbon dots: Antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are prepared using glucose as a carbon source (GCD) and doped with heteroatoms such as boron, sulfur, and nitrogen to enhance their functionality. FTIR and XPS results confirm doping of GCDs with heteroatoms, and their particle size is ~10 nm, as shown by TEM images. GCDs are very hydrophilic and stable in an aqueous solution, and nitrogen-doped GCD (NGCD) exhibits the highest polydispersity index of 0.274. The GCDs show excellent tunability of the PL emission with the variation in excitation wavelength. All the GCDs display excellent antioxidant function with the highest activity in the NGCD. The boron-doped GCD (BGCD) and sulfur-doped GCD (SGCD) exhibit more potent antibacterial activity against E. coli and Listeria monocytogenes. The NGCD is effective against both bacterial strains, showing the highest antibacterial activity. The NGCD exhibits potent antifungal activity against Ammophilus fumigatus, P. citrinum, C. albicans, and R. rubra, while the SGCD has a higher inhibitory effect on the growth of F. solani. Besides, more than 80% of the mouse fibroblast L929 cells are viable even when exposed to a high concentration of 500 μg/mL for 72 h, indicating low toxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: Earth is inhabited by many invisible microscopic organisms such as bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa, etc., and other organisms belonging to the higher kingdoms such as animals and plants. One of the well-known links between humans and microbes is that the human gut contains beneficial bacteria that aid the human digestive process [1]. However, not all microorganisms are beneficial, and many can cause severe infections to other organisms [2]. Besides, the number of species of these microorganisms continues to increase with changing environments and human habits. One such recent incident is the emergence of the SARS-CoV-2 virus causing coronavirus disease (COVID-19) and its mutation into several viral strains depending on different environmental conditions and exposure to various drugs inside the body [3]. These mutational processes and the emergence of new and powerful species are happening in viruses and other microbes. As a result, long-term continued use of antibiotics increases the difficulty of treating infectious diseases due to the emergence of multidrug-resistant microorganisms [4]. Bacteria and fungi are the most common disease-causing microorganisms in plants and animals. These microorganisms can cause a wide range of plant diseases (blast, sheath blight, etc.), impeding the growth and development of commercial and food crops, ultimately resulting in substantial economic losses [5].

Over the past decade, the utilization of functional nanoparticles has increased to address multi-drug resistance in microorganisms [4]. Many nanoparticles with potent antibacterial activity have been used due to their unique features, such as increased bacterial membrane permeability and reduced drug exudation compared to conventional antibiotics [6,7]. However, some of these antibacterial nanoparticles have other problems, such as high biological toxicity and not efficiently degrading under natural conditions, causing a series of secondary pollution and threats [8]. Therefore, developing biologically safe and degradable nanomaterials with a broad spectrum and high antimicrobial properties is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based spherical nanomaterials with typical sizes less than 10 nm [2]. Due to such small size, carbon dots exhibit unique photoluminescent properties that differentiate them from other carbon-based nanomaterials [9,10]. In addition to photoluminescence, CDs exhibit other beneficial properties such as water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, etc., unlike other commercially established high atomic weight metal and semiconductor quantum dots [11]. They also show high adsorption interactions with biological surfaces thanks to their small size with controllable surface functional groups [11,12]. These properties make them attractive materials to explore in biomedical applications such as bioimaging, biosensing, bio-labeling, photodynamic therapy, and drug delivery [13]. In addition to these biological properties, some recent studies have reported vigorous antioxidant activity [14] and moderate antimicrobial activity of CDs against various microorganisms, including bacteria [15,16], fungi [17], and viruses [18], opening up entirely new applicability for these substances [19].

The functional properties of CDs depend on the type of atoms present in the structure, which depends on the carbon source used. However, the atomic structure of CDs can be easily modified by methods such as surface functionalization and heteroatom doping. Heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron, etc., can be easily incorporated into the CDs core using reactants containing these atoms [20]. For example, boron-doped CDs can be prepared using boric acid as an additional reactant with a carbon source [21]. Boron, nitrogen, and sulfur were chosen as doping elements because of their low atomic weight, safe, economical, harmless to the environment, and less toxic than metal and semiconductor elements with high atomic weight. Moreover, these low atomic weight elements form part of the biogeochemical cycle in nature and, therefore, can be readily biodegradable [22–24]. Heteroatomic doping of CD cores may help to improve antibacterial and antioxidant properties while reducing toxicity. Heteroatom nitrogen‑carbon quantum dots have been reported as more efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction than homoatomic CDs [25]. Moreover, heteroatomic CDs have been reported to have more potent bactericidal activity [16]. The solid antibacterial effect of CDs with different functional groups was demonstrated, where the dots with the amine surface group showed an excellent antimicrobial effect against E. coli. The results indicated that E. coli facilitated their elimination once functionally distinct CDs had activated cell death [26]. However, to the best of our knowledge, systematic studies comparing the properties of CDs doped with different heteroatoms have not yet been reported, and there are few studies on the antifungal properties of pure CDs and CDs doped with various heteroatoms.

Therefore, the present study aims to synthesize, characterize, and evaluate the properties of CDs doped with three different heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and boron. CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal synthesis method using glucose as a carbon source. Glucose was chosen because it is economical, universal, and can generate pure carbonaceous CD cores and be an essential part of the human diet. The effect of heteroatom doping on the functional properties of the resulting CDs is estimated, and their functional and biological properties such as cytotoxicity, antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant activities have been evaluated.

CDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method using an aqueous glucose solution with dopant agents such as boric acid, sodium persulfate, and urea as boron, sulfur, and nitrogen sources, respectively, as shown schematically in Scheme 1 . Briefly, 1 g of glucose and 0.5 g of dopant formulation were dissolved with stirring in 60 mL of deionized water and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined stainless steel reactor (100 mL), and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove unreacted materials. Next, the supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, pore size: 0.22 μM, Germany) to remove impurities. The filtrate was then lyophilized to obtain a black powder of CDs and stored in the refrigerator before further testing. For comparison, glucose carbon dots (GCD) were also prepared using the same procedure without adding dopant. The prepared CDs were designated as GCD, BGCD, SGCD, and NGCD according to the dopant type. The prepared carbon dots were characterized using the methods described in the Supporting Information.

The morphological properties of CDs were evaluated using TEM, and the TEM images are shown in Fig. 1
(a–d). All CDs were spherical with uniform particle sizes of 5.9–11.0 nm (average diameter 8.9 ± 3.6 nm), 4.3–9.0 nm (average diameter 6.2 ± 2.4 nm), 4.9–9.7 nm (average diameter 6.9 ± 2.8 nm), and 3.3–8.2 nm (average diameter 5.6 ± 1.2 nm) for GCD, BGCD, SGCD, and NGCD, respectively. The TEM images clearly showed that the particle size distribution was in the quantum range, as previously reported [27]. Although the particle size has very small changes in doping, these subtle changes in the quantum domain cause significant changes in quantum confinement effects and, ultimately, PL emissions [28]. The morphology of all the CDs was spherical with almost no aggregation. Also, no visible difference in the particle morphology was observed with the doping.

Particles with a polydispersity index (PDI) value of less than 0.2 are generally considered monodisperse, indicating that the size distribution is narrow and not aggregated [29]. The PDI of GCD was 0.542, showing a wide particle size distribution. Except for SGCD, the PDI of CD was significantly reduced by doping with other elements. Sulfur-doped GCD (SGCD) showed a higher PDI of 0.642, probably due to aggregation formation. However, the PDI of GCD was 0.491, indicating that more monodisperse particles were formed. Among GCDs, NGCD (nitrogen-doped GCD) showed the lowest PDI value, indicating that the size distribution was narrower than that of other GCDs (Table S2).

The zeta potential of nanoparticles is used to determine suspension stability; the higher the zeta potential of nanoparticles, the more stable they are in suspension. CDs have a higher function than bulky particles due to their high surface-to-volume ratio [30], but their activity decreases when aggregated. Zeta potential analysis determines the surface charge of particles with high zeta potentials, which tend to repel each other and stably disperse and retain function [29,30]. The zeta potential of GCD was −5.74 mV, which decreased slightly to −5.52 and −3.81 mV, respectively, after doping with sulfur and nitrogen, indicating that they are less stable in the suspensions. On the other hand, the zeta potential of BGCD is −18 mV, indicating that BGCD is more stable in suspension than the other CDs. The difference in zeta potential values of GCDs doped with different atoms could be due to their electronegativity [31]. As electronegativity increases, so does the tendency to accept electrons. Carbon and sulfur have nearly similar electronegativity values (2.55 and 2.58, respectively), so the zeta potential values of GCD and SGCD are also the same. However, since nitrogen has a higher electronegativity value (3.04) than carbon, it is assumed that it accepts surface electrons from the GCD, neutralizes them, and reduces the negative zeta potential value by reducing the density. On the other hand, since boron has a lower electronegativity value (2.04) than carbon, the negative value of the zeta potential in BGCD increased [31].

XPS spectrum was performed to confirm elemental doping of the CDs structure. XPS spectra, binding energy levels (eV), and element content in atomic % of the CDs are shown in Fig. 1e and Table S1. The carbon content was decreased by doping heteroatoms in BGCD, SGCD, and NGCD, whereas the content of guest elements was similar in the three doped GCDs (11.04, 10.8, and 10.5%). The high oxygen content in SGCD (46.1%) and BGCD (36.03%) may suggest the formation of SOx groups (x = 2, 3, 4) in SGCD and BC2O/BCO2 in BGCD during hydrothermal synthesis. The survey resolution scan of the GCD revealed two typical peaks at 283.2 eV and 530.6 eV, which are assigned to the binding energies of C 1 s and O 1 s. These results confirmed that GCD is mainly composed of carbon and oxygen elements that bond through various single and double bonds, such as CC, CC, COH, COC, CO [32]. Besides the C 1 s and O 1 s peaks, the doped GCD showed new peaks at 191.8, 168.03, and 398.1 eV, consistent with the binding energies of the heteroatoms. Indeed, the B 1 s, S 2p, and N 1 s binding energy peaks in the XPS spectra of BGCD, SGCD, and NGCD support the presence of the element along with GCD. The B 1 s core energy level spectrum of BGCD contributes at 191.8 eV, corresponding to the BC bond in BC2O, BCO2, or maybe even BO in B2O3 [33]. Similarly, the S 2p peak of SGCD at 168.03 eV can be assigned to the CS bond in C-SO2, C-SO3, and C-SO4 species [34]. Moreover, as shown in the spectrum of NGCD, the characteristic peak at 398.05 eV matches N 1 s binding energy of nitrogen bonds (CN and NH) in amides or amino groups [35]. It is worth noting that the carbon content of GCD and NGCD was similar in both samples (69.9% and 68.2% of C1, respectively). Therefore, comparing the doped CDs, the contribution of C 1 s at 283.1 eV is highest in NGCD, confirming only the prominent roles of amide and amino groups in NGCD [36], which is supported by the results of FTIR analysis.

FTIR spectra of glucose and CDs are shown in Fig. 1f. Vibration stretching of OH, CH (methyl and methylene), CO, and C-O-C in glucose spectrum observed at 3323 cm−1, 2946 cm-1, and 2926 cm−1, 1747 cm−1, and 1101 cm−1, respectively. In addition, the CO stretching and O-H/C-H bending can also be observed in the form of weak intertwined peaks between 1440 and 1229 cm−1 [37]. In the case of GCD, the absorption bands related to the stretching vibration of OH (3262 cm−1), CO (1699 cm−1), and a combination of O-C-H/C-O-H vibration (1516–1368 cm−1) and C-H/O-H bonds deformation (1162 cm−1) are seen on the surface of the GCD [38]. The absorption peaks between 1068 and 960 cm−1 and 875–700 cm−1 regions can be attributed to stretching C-O/C-C and bending vibrations (out of plane) of aromatic CH [39]. The FTIR spectrum of BGCD, if we do not consider the peaks related to oxygenated groups, the distinct absorption peaks at 1394 and 1068 cm−1 can be attributed to stretching vibration of BO and BC bonds [40]. In the SGCD, the bands at 3227, 1705, 1419, and a weak peak at 1287 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching of OH, CO/CC, CH (aromatic), and COC bonds, respectively. Additionally, the characteristic bands at 1167, 1044, 867, and 571 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of CS, SO, and CC=S groups, in compliance with the XPS data indicated effective doping of sulfur in GCD [34]. Furthermore, in the FTIR spectrum of N-doped GCD (NGCD), the peaks in the fingerprint region can be ascribed to the vibration of CO (1080 cm−1), CC (1000 cm−1), and aromatic CH (845–770 cm−1) on the surface of the GCD [39]. Besides, the broadband absorption between 3650 and 2250 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of NH, OH, and CH on unsaturated carbon. The sharp band centered at 1575 and 1404 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching of CN bonds and carboxylic groups [41]. In addition to the XPS results, these results also confirm that the desired heteroatoms were successfully doped in the GCD structure.

UV–visible light absorption spectra were measured to evaluate the optical and visual properties of water-soluble CDs. Fig. 2a shows the color variation of GCDs and their absorption profile under daylight and UV-light (275–350 nm). The glucose solution was colorless under both white and UV light. In contrast, the brown color of the suspension of GCDs changed to an intense green light upon UV excitation, clearly due to the quantum confinement effect. A similar green-colored GCDs under UV light was observed in a previously published report [42]. The observed color development is due to light emission under UV light, commonly observed in CDs. However, the color intensity of CDs under UV varies greatly depending on the source and manufacturing method [32,43]. As shown in Fig. 2a, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) bands of all GCD solutions showed intense light absorption peaks at 295 nm due to the n-π* transition of CO or the C-OH bond of the GCDs [32]. This result is attributed to the carbonization of glucose, confirming the successful synthesis of GCD. However, doping of GCD showed little change in UV–vis light absorption and SPR profile for all types of GCDs. All doped GCDs showed an enhancement of the peak intensity with the shift of the maximum absorption wavelength of doped GCDs compared to the neat GCD. Interestingly, for NGCD, there was a more significant and sharp redshift and peak broadening at the peak maximum, most likely due to the N-substitution of the GCD. A similar broad absorption profile of N-doped GCD and redshift in absorption peak has also been reported [42,44]. The strong UV-light absorption properties of all GCDs are expected to show high UV-blocking properties, which is helpful for UV-blocking packaging applications.

The PL spectra of the diluted GCD samples at the excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. 2b–e. All GCDs showed strong and broad emission peaks in the green region, and the emission was tunable depending on the excitation wavelength. In the case of GCD, the excitation-dependent emission is a commonly observed phenomenon. This exclusive feature originates from multiple localize emission states in one particle and many PL bands [45]. Interestingly, increasing the excitation energy (330 to 430 nm) showed a clear and gradual bathochromic (red) shift of emission peak with lower peak intensity in all types of GCDs. In the GCD and NGCD, the highest PL emission intensity was observed ~450 nm at excitation of 350 nm and 390 nm, respectively, while for the BGCD and SGCD, the highest peak was around 400 nm at excitation of 320 nm. The peak shift trend was almost similar for the GCD (~450 to ~520 nm), BGCD (~400 to ~500 nm), and SGCD (~410 to ~590 nm). In contrast, interestingly, for the NGCD, a minor peak shift (~430 to ~460 nm) and abrupt-intensity variation took place, which is most likely due to the slight variation in size and quantum confinement effect due to the doping in GCD. As previously discussed, even subtle changes in the quantum domain cause significant changes in quantum confinement effects and ultimately PL emission [28]. Previously, a similar excitation-dependent PL emission peak was detected in the case of nitrogen-doped GCD [46]. It has been stated that doping has a considerable impact on the PL characteristics of quantum dots [45]. With an increased particle size of the CDs, the quantum confinement effect can be clarified through bandgap reduction due to π-electron delocalization [47]. Another plausible reason for excitation-dependent PL of GCDs could be the presence of many surface/edge functional groups and traps, molecular fluorophores, and graphitic cluster nanostructure, which may create many divergent energy states and consequently variable emission [45,47]. In fact, the PL mechanism of GCDs is a complex phenomenon. The passivated surface defects of carbon dots play an important role in varying emissions, and the degree of carbonization core-shell structure is thought to be an essential factor [48]. Various reports have previously confirmed that doping of CDs has a significant effect on the optical behavior (absorption and emission) of CDs depending on the source material and dopant type, which is consistent with the present results [36,49,50]. High-luminescence CDs can be applied for cell imaging, labeling, or other biomedical applications.

Antioxidants effectively neutralize free radicals or free radicals produced in the process of various detoxification reactions in our bodies [51]. The antioxidant activity of GCDs was assessed using the ABTS and DPPH methods, which are most commonly used to evaluate their free radical scavenging function. The results of the antioxidant activity of GCDs according to the concentration change are shown in Fig. 3
. The GCDs showed higher or equivalent radical scavenging activity than ascorbic acid at all concentrations tested and exhibited significant antioxidant activity within minutes of incubation in both methods. UV–vis spectra for all GCDs showed a substantial decrease in absorbance with increasing GCD concentration than the control solution. The characteristic ABTS and DPPH absorbance bands at 734 nm and 517 nm gradually disappeared with increasing GCD concentration. In particular, GCD and NGCD showed very high scavenging activity compared to ascorbic acid at all concentrations of 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL. The superior antioxidant activity of GCDs may be due to its surface functional groups participating in free radical scavenging to generate more stable products in the ABTS and DPPH assays [15,52]. The NGCD had the highest antioxidant activity, especially at low concentrations in the aqueous ABTS solution, which could be attributed to an electronegativity difference between nitrogen and carbon [53]. A high positive charge density of doping nitrogen on the carbon atom facilitates the interaction between the N-doped GCD and ABTS. However, the antioxidant activity was decreased in the BGCD and SGCD, which can be related to the generation of further OH− radicals. These kinds of radicals tend to recombine quickly to restore stability and prevent the quenching of oxidative free radicals [6,54]. It is worth mentioning that the free radical scavenging power of all doped CDs was higher in ABTS than in DPPH analysis, which can be due to the higher solubility and structural integrity of the CDs in the aqueous ABTS solution than in the methanolic DPPH solution [55,56].

The MIC and MBC/MFC of undoped and doped GCDs against pathogens microorganisms, Gram-positive bacteria (Listeria monocytogenes), Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli), mold (Aspergillus fumigatus, Fusarium solani, and P. citrinum), and yeast (Candida albicans and R. rubra) were determined using a 96-well microplate, and the results are shown in Table 1 . MIC is the concentration of GCDs at which bacterial growth is completely inhibited, and MBC/MFC is the minimum GCD concentration required to kill microbial cells completely. The GCD showed slight antimicrobial activity against both test bacteria, showing MIC/MBC values of 312/625 and 625/1250 μg/mL for L. monocytogenes and E. coli, respectively. By doping with B, S, and N atoms, GCD showed markedly increased antimicrobial activity. However, the effect was different depending on the type of dopant element. Although boron-doped GCD was not very effective against Gram-positive bacteria, it had a more substantial effect than the neat GCD on the growth of E. coli with MIC/MBC values of 156/312 μg/mL. On the other hand, SGCD and NGCD showed much higher antibacterial activity against both test bacteria, indicating that the antimicrobial function of GCD was improved by sulfur and nitrogen doping; however, NGCD exhibited more potent antibacterial activity than SGCD. Except for the BGCD, GCDs showed more potent antibacterial activity against Gram-positive than Gram-negative bacteria, mainly due to differences in the cell wall structure and affinity for interaction with GCDs. Gram-positive bacteria are more susceptible to the detrimental effects of ROS, primarily because they lack an outer membrane. Both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria consist of a hydrophilic cell wall made of peptidoglycan but with considerable differences in its overall structure. The Gram-positive bacteria possess a relatively simple cell wall structure comprising a thick layer of peptidoglycan on the outside, containing many negatively charged teichuronic acids and teichoic acids, followed by an inner membrane consisting of phospholipid bilayer structure. The outer peptidoglycan layer is relatively porous and can provide many anionic sites that aid in their electrostatic interaction with GCDs (Fig. 4a) [57]. Gram-positive bacteria infiltrate GCDs due to their simple cell wall structure, resulting in penetration and degradation. On the other hand, the cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria is a complex structure comprising a relatively thinner peptidoglycan layer sandwiched between two cell membranes composed of phospholipids and lipopolysaccharides on both inner and outer surfaces [58,59]. Due to this, the GCDs cannot directly interact with the peptidoglycan layer because of their inability to successfully penetrate the outer cell membrane due to the hydrophobicity of the latter. This results in a permeable barrier to foreign compounds, causing relative weak interaction and preventing GCD from entering bacterial cells, and hence a weaker antimicrobial effect (Fig. 4b) [36,60]. Indeed, the cell wall structure of Gram-positive bacteria aids in their electrostatic interaction with GCD (Fig. 4a) [57]. On the other hand, the cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria consists of an outer cell membrane composed of phospholipids and lipopolysaccharides. In addition, the intermittent peptidoglycan network sandwiched between the outer and inner membranes, which is a non-porous cell wall structure, cannot penetrate most molecules due to the hydrophobicity of the surface and creates a permeable barrier to foreign compounds, causing relative weak interaction and preventing GCDs from entering bacterial cells (Fig. 4b) [36,60]. It is also evident that the doping element plays an important role in antimicrobial activity. For example, it has been reported that sulfur-based antibacterial agents show higher antimicrobial activity against Gram-positive strains than against Gram-negative strains [22]. Similarly, SGCD showed lower MIC/MBC values for Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes) than Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli).

Neat GCD showed no antifungal activity, whereas doped GCD showed improved antifungal function against fungi (Aspergillus fumigatus, Fusarium solani, P. citrinum, C. albicans, and R. rubra) in the MIC/MBC-MFC test. Like the case of antibacterial activity, NGCD showed the highest antifungal activity, followed by SGCD and BGCD. SGCD was more active than BGCD to inhibit Aspergillus fumigatus, P. citrinum, C. albicans, and R. rubra, while SGCD showed the highest activity against Fusarium solani. Unlike bacteria, fungi are eukaryotic cells with different chemical compositions and structures, exhibiting an intermediate permeability higher than Gram-negative bacterial cell walls but lower than Gram-positive. Even though the fungal cell wall comprises of a single inner phospholipid bilayer membrane, similar to the Gram-positive bacteria, it is connected and covered by a complex network of chitin (unbranched polymer chains) cross-linked to additional membrane-bound proteins and β-1,3- and β-1-6-glucans, which limits its permeability to external molecules (Fig. 4c) [61].

The inhibitory effect of CDs was evaluated qualitatively on microbial growth using the agar well diffusion method. The well-diffusion assay shows the susceptibility of microorganisms to CDs through the size of the inhibition zone. The larger diameter of the inhibition zone is facilitated by the water-soluble capacity, dispersion, and high diffusion of CDs, essential for proper antibacterial ability [36]. The results of the well-diffusion test showing the inhibition zone are shown in Fig. 5 . For L. monocytogenes, the inhibition diameter of GCD was ~6 mm and increased to ~10, 13, and 17 mm by boron, sulfur, and nitrogen doping. Similarly, NGCD showed the highest activity against E. coli, followed by SGCD and BGCD, respectively. For Aspergillus fumigatus, P. citrinum, C. albicans, and R. rubra, undoped GCD showed the lowest inhibition zone (4–8 mm), whereas NGCD showed the highest diameter of inhibition (10–19 mm), followed by SGCD (10–13 mm) and BGCD (6–10 mm). The results of the well-diffusion test are in good agreement with those of the MIC test.

Compared to GCD, the much stronger antimicrobial activity of NGCD is probably due to specific functional groups such as amides and amines on the surface [36]. The N-doped GCD has been reported to contain chemical reactions related to electron-donating capacity [62,63]. The extra free electron in NGCD further enhances the electron-donating function, forms oxygen radicals, and enhances the electron transfer process. The higher antimicrobial activity of nitrogen-doped GCD than that of sulfur and boron-doped GCD is due to the positively charged N-moieties. The protonation of amines/amides groups facilitates GCD penetration into the negatively charged cell walls. It was previously shown that ions in the nitrogen complex act as free radicals, contributing to the inactivation of enzymes that require free hydroxyl groups to perform their functions [64]. It is reasonable to assume that nitrogen complexes can reduce polarity and increase the lipophilicity of the complexes, allowing them to more readily penetrate cell membranes and disrupt membrane structures through interactions with sensitive sites of enzymes [53]. It is also known that the fatty acid chains of the lipid bilayer and their interaction with the nitrogen group exhibit antifungal activity [65]. It is plausible to assume that the large amino/imine functional groups in NGCD interact with the anionic sites of lipid phosphate via electrostatic interaction and create a non-uniform surface charge on the lipid bilayer membrane perturbing membrane composition. This destabilization increases plasma membrane permeability, resulting in leakage of low molecular weight compounds (e.g., potassium ions) [66]. This potent antimicrobial activity by amine functional groups has been widely reported for chitosan, the only biopolymer with antimicrobial activity [67].

On the other hand, the main functional groups of SGCD are sulfonates and sulfonic acids, which bind to -SH functional groups of enzyme and generate ROS stress, which denatures proteins and lipids [36]. It has also been suggested that sulfur metabolites may react with cellular lipids to reduce total lipid content and release cellular components, which may inhibit the viability of microorganisms [65].

The XPS results indicate that the higher antimicrobial activity in BGCD than in undoped GCD may be related to the surface functional groups. Although the antibacterial mechanism of boron-doped GCD has not yet been elucidated, we would like to discuss a plausible explanation. Boron atoms are incorporated into the C lattice to form BC2O/BCO2 during hydrothermal synthesis. The deficient character of B3+ leads to the formation of OH due to the enhancement of Lewis acid on the BC2O/BCO2 surface. Once it occurs, OH− reacts with electron-hole pairs in an aqueous environment and turns into ·OH. These free radical groups are responsible for several aspects/characteristics of cell physiology, causing cell membrane destruction and lead to cell lysis [50]. The exact antimicrobial activity of doped GCDs has not yet been elucidated, and further investigation is needed to understand how they act against bacteria and fungi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of antibacterial activity of CD
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images of the GCD-treated bacteria further confirmed the antibacterial activity of the CDs (Fig. 6 ). The GCDs changed the microbe's morphology and induced severe cell membrane disruption. In contrast, the untreated control group showed normal growth with many cells. The bacteria treated with undoped GCD showed some damage to the cell surface and partial damage to the cell wall. However, the doped GCD-treated bacteria caused severe damage to the microbial cells and showed large-scale perturbation and cytoplasmic leakage in the periphery. Therefore, it can be concluded that doping GCDs with boron, sulfur, and nitrogen plays a vital role in disrupting the cell membrane structure, leading to a loss of bacterial integrity.

The ROS generating ability of GCD was measured to confirm the efficacy of antimicrobial activity using a modified method of the oxygen radical absorbance capability (ORAC) (Fig. 6). Depending on the specific functional group, the aqueous environment promotes the reaction to form O2, followed by hydroxide (OH−) and hydroperoxide (HO2 −) anions. ROS, such as O2 −, OH−, and HO2 −, attach to the microbial surface by electrostatic forces, interacting with proteins, lipids, and nucleotides to denature membrane proteins that cause severe cellular physiological damage and ultimately increase permeability and DNA proliferation associated with cell lysis [68,69]. ROS generation in the GCDs was measured using alkaline fluorescein to track the amount of green fluorescence produced by the oxidation of fluorescein. The prepared GCDs were added to the fluorescein solution, and the relative fluorescence amount was evaluated as a function of time by photoluminescence spectroscopy [70,71]. The pure fluorescence solution did not generate any ROS within one h. Still, the fluorescence peak intensity of the solution containing the undoped GCD was gradually decreased with increasing time, confirming the enhanced ROS generation by GCD. On the other hand, the low fluorescence signal of doped GCDs compared to the neat GCD indicated higher ROS generation over time and reached saturation level after 60 min. Overall, BGCD, SGCD, and NGCD showed high antibacterial efficiency through ROS generation in a time-dependent manner.

The use of CDs for food packaging or biomedical applications requires investigation of the cytotoxicity of CDs. The main goal of performing cytotoxicity tests is to determine biocompatibility. Moreover, the biocompatibility of CDs helps gain insight into whether the use of CDs-based nanocomposites may have potentially detrimental cytotoxic effects. CDs are most likely biocompatible nanomaterials because they are made using non-toxic raw materials and completely eco-friendly methods. A cell viability test was performed using the MTT test to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity of GCD, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . A variable concentration of GCDs (0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 mg/mL) was incubated in a fixed density of mouse fibroblast L929 cell lines, and the cell viability (%) was determined after 72 h. Cell density slightly increased in CDs and NGCD at 24 and 48 h, whereas a slight decrease was observed in BGCD and SGCD (data not shown). The cell viability after 72 h was varied depending on the type of GCDs and their concentration. The cell viability of doped GCD was significantly lower than that of undoped GCD, which was concentration-dependent. The cell viability of GCD decreased down to ~93% when tested at 0.5 mg/mL. Compared to the neat GCD, doped GCD showed lower cell viability, except for BGCD maintained high cell viability of more than 91%, SGCD and NGCD showed lower cell viability (80–85%). The order of cell viability or cytotoxicity of the doped GCD is consistent with the results of the antimicrobial activity of the CDs. It is worth noting that more than 80% of the cells survive for 72 h even at 500 μg/mL of CDs. The exact mechanism of action of the CDs on the mouse fibroblast L929 cell line is unknown, and further studies are needed to understand its activity. Moreover, the effectiveness of CDs may vary depending on the type of cell line used and the source of the CDs. However, the tested CDs have low toxicity against the mouse fibroblast L929 cell line. Overall, current results showed very low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility with CDs at low concentrations (0.1 mg/mL). Similar low cytotoxicity of citric acid-derived nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots against EA hy926 endothelial cells and nitrogen-doped carbon dots against Madin-Darby Canine Kidney and HeLa cells has been reported [52,72]. It has also been reported that for NGCD and SGCD, cell viability was maintained at >80% even at high concentrations of CDs (600 μg/mL) after an incubation of 48 h [73]. Various cytotoxicity studies have confirmed that CDs (with or without surface modification) exhibit very low toxicity [74,75]. Moreover, cytotoxicity studies of CDs performed to date have shown that in most cases, concentrations of 10–100 μg/mL cause only a slight loss in cell viability (~10–20%), which is suitable for cell labeling/staining purposes [76] and is also expected to be useful for food packaging applications.

Carbon dots (CD) are composed of a central carbon core with less than 10 nm and functional groups on the surface. In this study, carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method from a glucose source and doped with heteroatoms such as boron, sulfur, and nitrogen to enhance their functionality. Attempts have been made to synthesize CDs using various natural sources, such as biowastes. Still, the desired purity has not always been achieved due to the presence of various components in them. Glucose has the advantage of providing pure CDs with stable and strong luminescence since it is made up of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, and while synthesis, hydrogen and oxygen get eliminated as water and pure carbon remains [77]. Although many studies have reported CD's antibacterial and antioxidant properties, including the pure CDs and the CDs doped with B, S, and N. However, no study systematically compares the various physicochemical and functional properties of these doped CDs. This is the first study comparing the antibacterial, antifungal, antioxidant, and in vitro cytotoxicity of three heteroatom-doped CDs (B, S, N). The antibacterial and antifungal activity of CD and doped CD were evaluated against a wide range of microorganisms, including bacteria (L. monocytogenes and Escherichia coli), mold (Aspergillus fumigatus, Fusarium solani, and Penicillium citrinum), and yeast (C. albicans and Rhodotorula rubra) with three distinct methods such as MIC, agar well diffusion, and total colony counting method. N-doped CD showed the most potent activity among the tested CDs due to amines/amides groups, making them more likely to interact with bacterial cells and enter them. Besides, all CDs exhibited the highest antibacterial effects against Gram-positive bacteria, presumably due to the difference in cell wall composition affecting the entry of CD into bacterial cells. In addition, the SEM images of the CD-treated bacterial cells showed considerably distorted cell structure with a fewer bacteria population. Consequently, it is concluded that the antimicrobial activity of CD often depends on its small size, ROS generation, and interaction with the bacterial cell wall. Antioxidant activity is another important functional property of CD that opens up new applications for these quantum dots, such as biomedical or food packaging applications. We studied the concentration-dependent free radical scavenging activity of CD towards 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) free radicals. Also, N-doped CD showed the highest antioxidant activity at low concentrations in the aqueous ABTS solution due to the presence of amine groups along with hydroxyl groups on the surface, which also results in high dispersibility in aqueous solution. This study confirmed the negligible cytotoxicity of CD and heteroatom-doped CDs to L929 mouse fibroblasts. In a comparative in vitro cytotoxicity test, CD and all the heteroatom-doped CDs at concentrations of 10–100 μg/mL showed negligible cytotoxicity to L929 mouse fibroblasts, hence expected to be safe for biological applications. As a result, CDs with excellent functional properties, low cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility have a high potential for use in various research areas such as biomedical and food packaging.

The work aimed to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) doped with heteroatoms (i.e., boron, sulfur, and nitrogen) and compare their physicochemical and functional properties to determine the effect of doping. FTIR and XPS analysis confirmed successful heteroatom doping, and the size determined using TEM analysis was ~10 nm. The CDs were highly hydrophilic and stable in aqueous solutions. N-doped GCD (NGCD) showed the polydispersity index of 0.274, indicating a uniform particle size distribution. The functional properties of the CD, such as antioxidant and antibacterial activity, were greatly increased by doping with N, S, and B atoms. All doped CDs showed enhanced antimicrobial activity against various bacterial and fungal strains, and N-doped CD showed several times higher antimicrobial activity than the undoped CD. Moreover, NGCD exhibited the highest free radical scavenging activity determined by the ABTS method, much higher than standard ascorbic acid. Most importantly, the cytotoxicity of the CD was not significantly affected by doping. Therefore, carbon dots doped with low molecular weight heteroatoms with high functional properties and low cytotoxicity have great potential for active food packaging and biomedical applications.
